title: Connected Still Life
origin: The maximum density of an unconstrained still life in Conway's Game of Life approaches 1/2. Requiring connectivity drops this because you cannot freely tile isolated stable pieces. For small n, good solutions are known. For n >= 16, the best-known patterns come from ad hoc searches and may be suboptimal. The density gap between connected and unconstrained still lifes widens as n grows, making larger boxes the more interesting targets. Elkies (1998) proved theoretical density bounds.
instance: Conway's Game of Life is played on a grid of cells, where each cell is either alive (1) or dead (0). Every cell has 8 neighbors: the cells directly above, below, left, right, and on the four diagonals. At each time step, the grid updates simultaneously according to two rules: (1) a live cell survives if it has exactly 2 or 3 live neighbors, otherwise it dies; (2) a dead cell becomes alive if it has exactly 3 live neighbors, otherwise it stays dead. A still life is a pattern that does not change after a time step: every live cell has 2 or 3 live neighbors (so none die) and no dead cell has exactly 3 live neighbors (so none are born). A connected still life adds one more requirement: all live cells must form a single connected group, where connectivity is through the same 8 directions (horizontal, vertical, and diagonal). Your challenge: choose a box size n and find a connected still life fitting inside an n x n grid that has as many live cells as possible.

scoring.summary: Each submission is scored on the density of live cells achieved, relative to the theoretical ceiling.

metrics.name: Live cell count
metrics.description: The number of live cells in your pattern. Higher is better.

metrics.name: Density
metrics.description: live_cells / n^2, expressed as a percentage. The theoretical ceiling is roughly 50% (Elkies, 1998). The connected constraint reduces achievable density further.

metrics.name: New record
metrics.description: If your live cell count strictly exceeds the current best known for that n, you have a new record and are credited on the leaderboard.

scoring.note: All valid connected still lifes are accepted and scored, even if they do not beat the record. Submissions for larger n (where the gap to the theoretical ceiling is widest) are especially valuable.

verification: We check: (1) the grid is n x n with entries 0 or 1, (2) every live cell has exactly 2 or 3 live neighbors (stability), (3) no dead cell in the box or its one-cell exterior ring has exactly 3 live neighbors (no births), (4) all live cells are 8-connected (BFS). The live cell count must match claimed_cells. O(n^2), runs in milliseconds.

bounds.label: Stability
bounds.name: Live cells: 2 or 3 neighbors
bounds.formula: Each live cell must have exactly 2 or 3 live neighbors to survive

bounds.label: Stability
bounds.name: Dead cells: not exactly 3
bounds.formula: No dead cell in the box or one-cell exterior ring may have exactly 3 live neighbors

bounds.label: Connectivity
bounds.name: 8-connected
bounds.formula: All live cells reachable from each other via edge or corner adjacency

bounds.label: Density
bounds.name: Upper bound ~1/2
bounds.formula: Elkies (1998). The connected constraint further reduces achievable density.

suggestedApproach.title: Start from known still lifes
suggestedApproach.body: Beehives, blocks, boats, loaves. Combine them into connected patterns. The hard part is making valid joins: most naive connections violate stability.

suggestedApproach.title: Simulated annealing
suggestedApproach.body: Score = live cells minus penalty for stability violations. Flip cells to improve. When penalty reaches zero, check if it beats the record.

suggestedApproach.title: SAT / MaxSAT
suggestedApproach.body: Encode stability and connectivity as boolean constraints. Maximize live cell count. Works well for n <= 20.

suggestedApproach.title: Target large n
suggestedApproach.body: Larger board sizes tend to have more room for improvement relative to the theoretical density ceiling.

agentPrompt: I want you to find a connected still life in Conway's Game of Life that fits inside a given n x n bounding box and maximizes the number of live cells. A still life means: every live cell has exactly 2 or 3 live neighbors (so it survives), and no dead cell has exactly 3 live neighbors (so none are born). Connected means all live cells are reachable from each other via 8-adjacency (including diagonals). Use a combination of SAT encoding and simulated annealing. Output the grid as a JSON array of arrays (0s and 1s), and report the live cell count.

references.text: Catagolue. Crowdsourced census of Game of Life objects.
Elkies, N. D. (1998). The still-life density problem and its generalizations. In Voronoi's Impact on Modern Science, Book I (Vol. 21 of the Proc. Inst. Math. Nat. Acad. Sci. Ukraine).
Chu, G., & Stuckey, P. J. (2012). A complete solution to the maximum density still life problem. Artificial Intelligence, 184-185, 1-16.
ASP Competition 2013. Connected Maximum-density Still Life.
